di: Known since ~6,000 BCE
ff: One of the few elements found pure in nature; valued since prehistoric times

di: Known since ~4,000 BCE
ff: Best electrical conductor of all elements

di: Known since ~8,000 BCE
ff: One of the first metals used by humans, over 10,000 years ago

di: Known since ~4th century BCE (India)
ff: Hardest natural substance on Earth — pure carbon atoms arranged in a lattice

di: Used prehistorically; named 1789 by Abraham Werner
ff: Allotrope of diamond — same element (carbon), completely different properties

di: Known since antiquity (mentioned in the Bible)
ff: Found around volcanic vents and hot springs; burns with a blue flame

di: Named 1748 by Antonio de Ulloa (Spanish Navy)
us: Jewelry, catalytic converters, lab equipment, cancer drugs
ff: Rarer than gold — its entire annual production would fill less than one swimming pool

di: Known since prehistoric times
ff: Most abundant mineral in Earth's continental crust

di: Known since ~2,500 BCE (ancient Egypt)
ff: Purple color comes from iron impurities; historically believed to prevent intoxication

di: Known since ~7,000 BCE (Mesopotamia)
ff: Pink hue caused by trace amounts of titanium, iron, or manganese

di: Used as tools since ~700,000 BCE (Stone Age)
ff: Not a true mineral — a natural volcanic glass sharper than surgical steel

di: First described 1801 by Martin Klaproth
ff: Most abundant mineral group on Earth's surface, forming ~60% of the crust

di: Used since ancient times; formally described 1850
ff: Splits into perfect transparent sheets — used as 'isinglass' windows before glass was available

di: Described 1847 by J.F.L. Hausmann
ff: Used for potassium-argon dating to determine the age of rocks

di: Known since ancient times; formally described ~1790
ff: Makes up most of Earth's mantle — also found in meteorites and on the surface of Mars

di: Known since ~3,100 BCE (ancient Egypt)
ff: Garnet group has over 20 species; used as abrasive since the Bronze Age

di: Known since ~1,500 BCE; prized by ancient Romans
ff: Pure topaz is colorless — blue topaz is usually colorless topaz treated with radiation

di: Named 1703 (from Sinhalese 'turmali')
ff: Can display two or more colors in one crystal — watermelon tourmaline is green outside, pink inside

di: Mined since ~4,000 BCE (ancient Egypt, Sinai)
ff: Colored green by chromium impurities; Cleopatra's favorite gemstone

di: Known since ~300 BCE; named by ancient Romans
ff: Blue-green color caused by iron; the name means 'water of the sea' in Latin

di: Used since ancient times; described 1546 by Agricola
us: Talcum powder, paint, ceramics, paper, plastics
ff: Softest known mineral — hardness 1 on the Mohs scale; feels soapy to the touch

di: Described 1789 by Abraham Werner
us: High-temperature ceramics, spark plugs, refractory materials
ff: Unusual mineral with different hardness in different directions — 4.5 along the crystal, 7 across it

di: Known since antiquity; oldest Earth crystals ~4.4 billion years old
us: Gemstones, ceramic glazes, nuclear fuel, refractory materials
ff: Oldest mineral ever found on Earth — some zircon crystals are 4.4 billion years old

di: First described 1770, Labrador, Canada
ff: Shows labradorescence — a spectacular play of iridescent colors caused by light interference

di: Revered in China for 5,000+ years
ff: Sacred in Chinese culture for over 5,000 years; not to be confused with nephrite, the other 'jade'

co: Steel grey to black; red streak
di: Used as red ochre pigment since ~40,000 BCE
ff: Leaves a distinctive red-brown streak; the red color of Mars comes from hematite dust

di: Known since ~600 BCE (Magnesia, Greece)
us: Iron ore, compasses, magnetic recording media, ferrofluids
ff: Natural magnet — the original compass mineral; some bacteria produce tiny magnetite crystals to navigate

di: Named 1798 by Heinrich Klaproth
ff: Ruby and sapphire are both corundum — only iron and titanium separate a sapphire from a ruby

di: Known since ~2,500 BCE (ancient Myanmar)
ff: Red color from chromium impurities; the world's first laser (1960) used a synthetic ruby rod

di: Prized since ~800 BCE (ancient Persia)
us: Gemstones, watch crystals, smartphone screens, industrial bearings
ff: Second hardest gem after diamond; 'fancy sapphires' can be pink, yellow, or orange

di: Described 1803 by Werner & d'Andrada
us: Titanium production, white pigment (TiO₂), solar cells, welding electrodes
ff: Provides the white pigment in white paint — covers more surface area than any other pigment

di: Used since ~3,500 BCE (Bronze Age, Turkey and Cornwall)
us: Primary tin ore (bronze, solder, tin cans)
ff: Tin from cassiterite enabled the Bronze Age — the most important ore of tin for 5,000 years

di: First described 1546 by Georgius Agricola
ff: The Black Prince's 'Ruby' in the British Crown Jewels is actually a red spinel

di: Known since ancient times; defines Mohs hardness 3
us: Limestone, cement, lime, antacids, paper filler
ff: Most common carbonate mineral; when pure, splits light into double refraction (Iceland spar)

di: Described 1791 by Déodat de Dolomieu (French geologist)
us: Building stone, magnesium source, steel-making flux
ff: The Dolomites mountains in Italy are named after this mineral; entire mountain ranges are made of it

di: Used since ~4,000 BCE (ancient Egypt as pigment and copper ore)
us: Gemstone, green pigment, copper ore, decorative objects
ff: Striking banded green stone; used as pigment by ancient Egyptians for eye shadow

di: Used since ~3,000 BCE (ancient Egypt and China)
pr: Limited — mostly byproduct of copper mining
us: Blue pigment, gemstone, minor copper ore
ff: Often found intergrown with malachite; one of the most important blue pigments in medieval European art

di: Described 1813 by Hausmann
ff: Argentina's national gemstone — the Incas believed it was condensed blood of their ancient kings

di: Named 1797 from Molina de Aragón, Spain
ff: Same composition as calcite but different crystal structure — pearls and coral are made of aragonite

di: Named 1832 after James Smithson (founder of the Smithsonian)
ff: Named after James Smithson, founder of the Smithsonian Institution, who first identified it as a zinc carbonate

di: Known since ancient times (Greek: pyr = fire)
us: Sulfuric acid production, iron ore, formerly fire-starting
ff: 'Fool's Gold' — deceived many prospectors; also caused shipwrecks by spontaneously combusting in damp holds

di: Used since ~5,000 BCE (ancient Rome for pipes and paint)
us: Lead ore (primary source), crystal radios, radiation shielding
ff: Densest common mineral; the first semiconductors used in crystal radios were galena crystals

di: Named 1725; used since antiquity as copper ore
pr: Dominant source of global ~22 million tonnes/year copper
ff: Principal ore of copper worldwide; found in nearly every copper deposit on Earth

di: Named 1847 (Greek: sphaleris = treacherous)
us: Zinc ore, germanium and indium source (electronics)
ff: Primary source of zinc; also yields rare metals like indium used in smartphone touchscreens

di: Used since ~4,000 BCE as red pigment (vermilion)
ff: The only significant ore of mercury; its brilliant red pigment was used in Roman frescoes — dangerously toxic

di: Named 1845 after Ignaz von Born (Austrian mineralogist)
ff: 'Peacock ore' — tarnishes to spectacular iridescent purple, blue, and green when exposed to air

di: Described 1778 by Carl Wilhelm Scheele
us: Molybdenum source (steel alloys for jet engines), solid lubricant
ff: Softest metal sulfide; looks like graphite and was mistaken for it — molybdenum strengthens steel for jet engines

di: Used since ~3,000 BCE (kohl eyeliner in ancient Egypt)
us: Antimony ore, fireworks, safety matches, flame retardants
ff: Longest natural metallic crystals — can grow to 60 cm; used as kohl eyeliner in ancient Egypt and the Middle East

di: Used since antiquity in China and the Middle East
us: Formerly in fireworks and insecticides (highly toxic arsenic content)
ff: Breaks down in light to yellow orpiment — ancient Chinese medicine used it to repel evil spirits despite its arsenic content

di: Named 1868 (Greek: pyrrhos = flame-colored)
pr: Part of nickel and copper ore production
us: Nickel and sulfur source, sometimes pentlandite host
ff: Weakly magnetic iron sulfide — the only magnetic sulfide mineral; found in meteorites from Mars

di: Used since ~8,000 BCE (Neolithic salt trade)
us: Table salt, food preservation, road de-icing, chlorine production
ff: One of the oldest traded commodities — Roman soldiers were partly paid in salt (origin of the word 'salary')

co: All colors, often purple or green
di: Named 1546 by Georgius Agricola (Latin: fluere = to flow)
us: Flux in steel-making, optical lenses, HF acid, fluoride in toothpaste
ff: Defines hardness 4 on the Mohs scale; the word 'fluorescence' was coined from fluorite, which glows under UV light

di: Described 1832 (named after Franciscus Sylvius)
ff: Tastes more bitter than rock salt; mined in enormous quantities to feed the world's crops

di: Named 1786 by Abraham Werner (Greek: apatao = to deceive)
us: Phosphate fertilizer, hydroxylapatite for bone grafts, dental enamel
ff: Defines hardness 5 on the Mohs scale; your teeth and bones are partly made of a form of apatite

di: Mined since ~6,000 BCE (Sinai Peninsula, Egypt)
us: Gemstones, decorative art, Native American jewelry
ff: One of the oldest gemstones — mined in the Sinai by Egyptians 6,000 years ago; sacred to Native Americans

co: Colorless fresh, turns blue-green on exposure
di: Named 1817 after J.H. Vivian (British mineralogist)
us: Blue pigment, minor iron ore, collectors' mineral
ff: Starts colorless but turns intensely blue-green on exposure to light due to iron oxidation

di: Named 1813 (Greek: pyr = fire, morphe = form)
pr: Minor — mainly byproduct of lead mining
ff: One of the most brightly colored lead minerals; its name means 'fire form' as it takes a rounded crystal shape when heated

di: Used since ~5,000 BCE (ancient Egyptian plaster)
us: Drywall (plasterboard), plaster of Paris, soil conditioner, cement
ff: Defines hardness 2 on the Mohs scale; when heated it loses water and becomes plaster of Paris

di: Described 1605 (Greek: barys = heavy)
us: Oil drilling mud, X-ray contrast agent, paints, sound insulation
ff: Surprisingly heavy for a non-metallic mineral — its weight makes it ideal for oil drilling fluids to control pressure

di: Named 1798 (Latin: caelestis = heavenly, for its blue color)
us: Strontium production, fireworks (red color), metal alloys
ff: Primary source of strontium — gives fireworks and emergency flares their brilliant crimson-red color

di: Named 1804 by Abraham Werner (Greek: an-hydor = waterless)
us: Cement additive, soil conditioner, filler in paper and paint
ff: Chemically identical to gypsum without water; slowly absorbs water from air and expands, causing buildings to crack

di: Named 1821 after Carl Wilhelm Scheele (discoverer of tungsten)
us: Tungsten source for light bulb filaments, cutting tools, armor-piercing ammunition
ff: Fluoresces brilliant blue-white under ultraviolet light; miners use UV lamps to locate it in the dark

di: Named 1845 (origin of the symbol W for tungsten)
us: Tungsten ore, vibration motors in phones, armor-piercing projectiles
ff: 'Conflict mineral' — mined in war zones; the tiny vibration motor in your phone likely contains tungsten from this mineral

di: Used since ~4th century BCE (China and Tibet)
us: Detergent booster, glass production, ceramics, soldering flux
ff: Mined extensively in Death Valley — the famous 20-mule team wagons hauled borax out of the California desert

di: Named 1850 after Georg Ludwig Ulex (German chemist)
ff: Known as 'TV rock' — its parallel fibers transmit images like fiber optics; you can read text through a piece

di: Named 1806 after Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
us: Yellow ochre pigment, iron ore, rust patina on weathered steel
ff: One of the most widespread iron minerals; 'yellow ochre' used in cave paintings 40,000 years ago is goethite

co: Green in daylight, red under incandescent light
di: Discovered 1830 in the Ural Mountains (named for Tsar Alexander II)
ff: Changes color from green to red depending on the light source — rarer than diamonds; a 1-carat natural alexandrite can cost more than a diamond

di: Named 1790 by Abraham Werner
ff: Third hardest natural gem after diamond and corundum; cat's-eye chrysoberyl shows a moving line of light (chatoyancy)

co: White to black with play of color
di: Used since ~4,000 BCE; named by Pliny the Elder (~50 CE)
ff: Play-of-color called 'opalescence' is caused by diffraction of light through tiny silica spheres stacked in perfect layers

di: Named 1845 (Greek: chalkos = copper)
us: High-grade copper ore (79.8% copper by weight)
ff: One of the most profitable copper ores; a supergene mineral formed near the surface when descending fluids enrich copper deposits

di: Named 1845 after Franz X. von Wulfen (Austrian mineralogist)
us: Minor molybdenum ore, highly prized collectors' mineral
ff: Some of the most aesthetically striking crystals in mineralogy — thin orange tablets described as 'stained glass windows in stone'

co: Pink to red with black veins
di: Named 1819 (Greek: rhodon = rose)
ff: Pink color from manganese; the black veins are manganese oxide dendrites — Russia designated it as the national gemstone

di: Named ~300 BCE by Theophrastus (Greek: chrysos = gold, kolla = glue)
ff: Often confused with turquoise; Queen Cleopatra reportedly wore chrysocolla jewelry — Plutarch called her 'the chrysocolla woman'

di: Discovered 1944 by Ken-ichi Sugi (Japanese petrologist)
ff: One of the few truly purple gemstones; discovered only in 1944 and available in gem quality only since the 1970s

di: Discovered 1967 near Mount Kilimanjaro, Tanzania
ff: Found only near Mount Kilimanjaro, Tanzania — geologists say conditions for its formation were so rare it will never be found elsewhere

di: Mined since ~1,500 BCE (Zabargad Island, Red Sea)
ff: One of few gems that forms in only one color; ancient Egyptians called it the 'gem of the sun' and believed it protected against evil

di: Used by Vikings ~1,000 CE as polarizing lens
ff: Viking compass stone — its polarizing effect helped Norse sailors navigate by sky light even on overcast days

di: Named 1832 from the Spessart region, Bavaria, Germany
ff: The 'mandarin garnet' variety from Namibia displays an intense orange glow; manganese gives it its vivid color

di: Named 1795 by Abraham Werner after Mount Vesuvius
pr: Limited — mostly ornamental and collectors' mineral
ff: First found in the lavas of Mount Vesuvius, ejected during the same eruption that buried Pompeii in 79 AD
